---
title: "What does it take to stop an Aucklander?"
subtitle: "Auckland CBD Pedestrian Analysis"
author: "Jeff He"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
---

# Intro
Auckland is famous for its highly unpredictable weather. On a sunny day, it can start raining out of nowhere, then immediately turn sunny again. This is what most Aucklanders describe as the typical "Auckland weather". Coming from China, my definition of a busy street is very different from NZ's standard. When I first moved to Auckland, the CBD already felt very quiet by comparison. Given the typical "Auckland weather". Wouldn't Auckland CBD turn into a ghost town as soon as it rained? 

To prove my assumption. I have used the pedestrian sensor data from Heart of City Auckland, in conjunction with Open-Meteo's Weather API. I expected to see a significant reduction in pedestrians when it is raining or windy. 

But it turns out I was dead wrong...

# Data Analysis
## 1. Setting up
Let's start by loading and fixing our data. Here are some of the problems I found:

| Data Issue | Action Taken |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Spelling error in sensor name** | Renamed "59 High Stret" to "59 High Street" |
| **Missing Data / NA values** | Dropped rows missing Date or Time |
| **Irrelevant entries** | Filtered out "Daylight Savings" timestamp entries |
| **Date not loaded as the corrected datatype** | Created a new column "Datetime" using "Date" and "Time". Ensuring it is the right datatype. |

```{python}
import pandas as pd
 
ped = pd.read_csv(r"data/akl_ped-2024.csv") # Loading dataset
ped.rename(columns={"59 High Stret":"59 High Street"}, inplace=True) # Correcting street name

ped = ped.dropna(subset=["Date", "Time"]) # Removing NA
ped = ped[ped["Date"] != "Daylight Savings"] # Removing daylight saving data entry

sensor_cols = ped.columns[2:] # Slicing the date and time column from original dataset. Keeping only sensors. 

ped["Datetime"] = pd.to_datetime(ped["Date"] + " " + ped["Time"].str.split("-").str[0], dayfirst=True,format='mixed') # Merging date and time


# Creating new columns
ped["Hour"] = ped["Time"].str.split(":").str[0].astype(int)
ped["Month"] = ped["Datetime"].dt.month
ped["DayOfWeek"] = ped["Datetime"].dt.dayofweek
```

Here is the list of sensors we will be using.
```{python}
sensor_cols
```

Before we do anything more. It may be a good idea to visualise our data first.
```{python}
import folium
from folium.plugins import HeatMap

sensor_means = ped[sensor_cols].mean() # Computing the average ped count for each sensors

sensor_means = sensor_means.reset_index() # Converting index column to normal column
sensor_means.columns = ['Sensor', 'Avg_Count'] # Renaming the column

geo_location = pd.read_csv(r"data/akl_ped-Geodata.csv") # Loading data with geo coordinate for each sensor

map_data = pd.merge(sensor_means, geo_location, left_on='Sensor', right_on='Address') # Merging the data

ped_avg_data = [[row['Latitude'], row['Longitude'], row['Avg_Count']] for index, row in map_data.iterrows()] # Creating list formated data for folium

m = folium.Map(location=[-36.8485, 174.7633], zoom_start=15, tiles="CartoDB positron") # Creating a base map focused on Queen St

HeatMap(ped_avg_data, radius=25, blur=15, gradient={0.4: 'blue', 0.65: 'lime', 1: 'red'}).add_to(m) # Adding the HeatMap layer to the folium map


# Adding markers data popup.
for index, row in map_data.iterrows():
    folium.CircleMarker(
        location=[row['Latitude'], row['Longitude']],
        radius=5,
        popup=f"{row['Sensor']}: {int(row['Avg_Count'])} avg/hr",
        color='black',
        fill=True,
        fill_opacity=0.7
    ).add_to(m)
# m.save("Ped Avg Count By Sensor Spatial Map.html")
m
```

We can see that 30 Queen Street has the highest average pedestrian per hour of 734, and 1 Courthouse Lane has the lowest average pedestrian per hour count of 51. However without further context. We cannot generate additional meaningful insights.

Now let's create an API call to Open-Meteo to pull the weather data for our analysis. The weather data I am requesting are:

| Parameter | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **rain** | Measurement of rain in mm. |
| **wind_speed_10m** | Wind speed in kmph measured at 10 metre height. |
| **weather_code** | Weather coding based on WMO (World Meteorological Organization) 4677 code. |

```{python}
# Parameters for making API call to Open-Meteo
LATITUDE = 36.8509
LONGITUDE = 174.7645
START_DATE = "2024-01-01"
END_DATE = "2024-12-31"
PARAMETERS = ["rain", "wind_speed_10m", "weather_code"]

import requests

weather_api = f"https://archive-api.open-meteo.com/v1/archive?latitude={LATITUDE}&longitude={LONGITUDE}&start_date={START_DATE}&end_date={END_DATE}&hourly={','.join(PARAMETERS)}&timezone=auto" # Creating API link
response = requests.get(weather_api) # Requesting data
data = response.json() # Formatting the data to json.

weather_df = pd.DataFrame(data['hourly']) # Converting the values for hourly weather to dataframe.

weather_df['time'] = pd.to_datetime(weather_df['time']) # Updating the datatype.
weather_df.head()
```

It will be a good idea to categorise these quantitative values now for future analysis. So lets define some functions to do so.

```{python}
def weather_code_mapping(code):
    """
    Maps WMO 4677 weather codes to simplified pedestrian-focused categories.

    Parameters
    ----------
    code : int
        WMO Weather interpretation code.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        Simplified weather condition (e.g., 'Clear/Dry', 'Heavy Rain', 'Storm').
    """
    if code <= 3:
        return "1. Clear/Dry" # Adding number infront for sorting
    elif (4 <= code <= 19) or (30 <= code <= 49):
        return "2. Low Visibility (Fog/Haze)"
    elif code in [20, 21, 50, 51, 58, 60, 61, 80]:
        return "3. Light Rain/Drizzle"
    elif code in [62, 63, 64, 65, 81, 82, 91, 92]:
        return "4. Heavy Rain"
    elif code in [17, 18, 29, 95, 96, 97, 99]:
        return "5. Storm/Thunderstorm"
    else:
        return "6. Other"

def wind_speed_mapping(speed):
    """
    Categorizes wind speed using a simplified scale inspired by the Beaufort Scale.

    Parameters
    ----------
    speed : float
        Wind speed in km/h.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        Wind intensity category from 1 (Light) to 5 (Storm).
    """
    if speed < 12:
        return "1. Light (0-11)"
    elif speed < 29:
        return "2. Moderate (12-28)"
    elif speed < 50:
        return "3. Strong (29-49)" 
    elif speed < 75:
        return "4. Gale (50-74)"
    else:
        return "5. Storm (75+)"



# Applying the mapping fucntions
weather_df["weather"] = weather_df["weather_code"].apply(weather_code_mapping)
weather_df["wind"] = weather_df["wind_speed_10m"].apply(wind_speed_mapping)
weather_df
```

Now let's merge the our weather dataset and our pedestrian count dataset together.

```{python}
merged = pd.merge(ped, weather_df, left_on="Datetime", right_on="time",how="inner") # Merging the dataset
ped_long = pd.melt(
    merged,
    id_vars=["Datetime", "Hour", "DayOfWeek", "weather", "wind"],
    value_vars=sensor_cols.tolist(),
    var_name="location",
    value_name="count"
) # Melting the dataset from wide format to long format
ped_long
```

Finnaly, lets start our analysis!

## 2. Visualising the Data

Let's begin by seeing what variable is correlated with pedestrian count.

It would appear that hour is highly correlated with the total pedestrian count. This is no surprise. CBD is where people conduct business. We should expect lower pedestrian counts outside of business hour. The typical work hour is 9am to 5pm. Since humans cannot teleport from one place to another yet (Not that I know of). I will extend the interval by one hour on both the start and end, as we should expect to see an increase in pedestrian count an hour prior/after start/end time. Thus we limit our analysis to 8am - 6pm. This should allow us to focus the effect coming other variables.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Correlation Heatmap"
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8)) 
merged['Total_Count'] = merged[sensor_cols].sum(axis=1) # Merging the count by row. To obtain hourly ped sum.

overall_corr = merged[['Total_Count', 'rain', 'wind_speed_10m', 'Hour']].corr() # Generating corrlation matrix with the selected variables
sns.heatmap(overall_corr, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f") # Displaying the corrlation matrix
plt.title("Auckland CBD Pedestrian Correlation Analysis") # Adding title to the plot
plt.show()
```

This data showing average pedestrian count by weekday makes sense. As Mon to Fri is business day, hence it has higher pedestrian count during working hours. Fri and Sat has a higher non-working hour pedestrian count, as people tend to relax and hangout after a long week of work. Sunday has the lowest pedestrian count in the non-working hour because people can't stay out late for Monday's work. 

Now let's dive into the main topic. Let's kick things off with analysing the wind speed. Auckland CBD has significantly lower skyscraper count than other megacities, and New Zealand is an island country. Hence the pedestrians faces from higher wind speed all the time. Could this dramatically affect the pedestrian count?

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count By Weekday (Working Hours vs Non-working Hours)"

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,5))

working_hour_df = ped_long[(ped_long["Hour"] >= 8) & (ped_long["Hour"] < 18)]  # Non inclusive of 18. Because due to the way dataset is set up. 18 is 18:00-18:59.
working_hour_mean = working_hour_df.groupby("DayOfWeek")["count"].mean() # Obtaining weekday average

non_working_hour_df = ped_long[~((ped_long["Hour"] >= 8) & (ped_long["Hour"] < 18))]
non_working_hour_mean = non_working_hour_df.groupby("DayOfWeek")["count"].mean()

# Plotting the two data to the same axis
ax.plot(working_hour_mean.index, working_hour_mean, label='Working Hours (8am-6pm)') 
ax.plot(non_working_hour_mean.index, non_working_hour_mean, color='aqua', label='Non-Working Hours (6pm-8am)')

ax.set_title("Average Pedestrian Count By Weekday")
ax.set_ylabel("Pedestrian count")
ax.legend() # Displaying legend
plt.xticks([0,1,2,3,4,5,6], ['Mon', 'Tue', 'Wed', 'Thu', 'Fri', 'Sat', 'Sun']) # Setting up custom x labels.

plt.show()
```

While the mean for pedestrian count across wind categories appears to be similar. However there is a noticeable reduction in the number of outliers for storm wind speed. While this suggest that wind speed has some impact on pedestrian count, but this more likely due to the smaller sample size. Looking back at the whole dataset. The average wind speed is 31 km/h. It is no wonder why wind speed has such a little impact on pedestrian count. Because Aucklanders are already used to strong wind!

Let's then analyse the weather. Rain should make a pretty big impact on pedestrian count. When I first moved to Auckland. One thing I noticed is that people don't tend to keep an umbrella in their bag. So you often see people running in the rain. Soaking wet. So could this be what makes the CBD a ghost town?

```{python}
def display_box_plot(df, column, title):
    """
    Generates a boxplot to visualize pedestrian count distribution across categories.

    Parameters
    ----------
    df : pd.DataFrame
        The dataset containing pedestrian counts and categorical variables.
    column : str
        The column name to categorize the x-axis (e.g., 'wind' or 'weather').
    title : str
        The title for the generated plot.

    Returns
    -------
    tuple (pd.Series, pd.Series)
        A tuple containing the value counts of the categories and the mean counts.
    """
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12,8))
    order = sorted(df[column].unique()) # Organising the order for better visual

    sns.boxplot(data=df, x=column, y="count", hue=column, ax=ax, order=order)
    ax.set_ylabel("Pedestrian count")
    ax.set_title(f"{title}")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    return df[column].value_counts(), df.groupby(column)["count"].mean() # Returning the distribution and mean for each category
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count By Wind (8am-6pm)"

distribution, mean = display_box_plot(working_hour_df, "wind", "Average Pedestrian Count By Wind (8am-6pm)")
print(distribution)
print(mean)
```

No. Again we are seeing similar trends as the wind speed. While the mean pedestrian count appears similar across all four weather scenario. But there is a noticeable redunction in outliers for both Light Rain/Drizzle and Heavy Rain compared to Clear/Dry. It appears that Aucklanders are very stubborn. No matter if it is pouring outside, or there is a storm. Aucklanders will always get to where they need to be, whether dry or completely soaking wet. This is very inspirational!

Let's put Aucklanders up to the final test! Let's test the scenario where it is heavy rain with a storm. Will this be the final blow to take down the almighty Aucklanders?

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count By Weather (8am-6pm)"
distribution, mean = display_box_plot(working_hour_df, "weather", "Average Pedestrian Count By Weather (8am-6pm)")
print(distribution)
```

No significant difference in the mean again! While the outliers have significantly reduced. But this could be due to the underlying sample has reduced from my rigorous scenario. Perhaps the extreme weather has altered the underlying heat pattern?

```{python}
def classify_stress(row):
    """
    Categorizes individual rows into weather stress scenarios based on extreme conditions.

    Parameters
    ----------
    row : pd.Series
        A single row from the pedestrian-weather dataframe.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        A category label (e.g., 'Baseline', 'Storm Only', 'Both').
    """
    is_windy = row['wind'] == "5. Storm (75+)" # Creating boolean series
    is_rainy = row['weather'] == "4. Heavy Rain"
    
    if is_windy and is_rainy:
        return "4. Both (Storm & Heavy Rain)"
    elif is_windy:
        return "3. Storm Only"
    elif is_rainy:
        return "2. Heavy Rain Only"
    else:
        return "1. Baseline (Clear/Calm)"

working_hour_df["weather_stress"] = working_hour_df.apply(classify_stress, axis=1) # Axis = 1 applying the function to each row.
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count By Scenarios (8am-6pm)"
distribution, mean = display_box_plot(working_hour_df, "weather_stress", "Average Pedestrian Count By Scenarios (8am-6pm)")
print(distribution)
```

Looking at the heat map. They all look identical. What if I told you they are actually the same image? That would make a hilarious joke but no. If you look closely, only in high wind speed (Storm) we can see an arise of a new hotspot in the central of CBD. This could be again due to sampling, or because central CBD has a cluster of skyscrapers. The wind is blocked by the skyscrapers altering pedestrian movement pattern. But that goes beyond the study of this article.

```{python}

def display_spatial_map(df, column, geo_df, display_marker=False):
    """
    Generates an interactive Folium HeatMap with toggleable layers for different scenarios.

    Parameters
    ----------
    df : pd.DataFrame
        The dataset containing location names and pedestrian counts.
    column : str
        The column to split the layers by (e.g., 'weather_stress').
    geo_df : pd.DataFrame
        Dataframe containing 'Latitude', 'Longitude', and 'Address' for sensors.
    display_marker : bool, optional
        Whether to add individual circle markers for each sensor, by default False.

    Returns
    -------
    folium.Map
        An interactive map object.
    """
    import folium
    from folium.plugins import HeatMap
    import pandas as pd

    m = folium.Map(location=[-36.8485, 174.7633], zoom_start=15, tiles="CartoDB positron") # Creating base map

    scenarios = sorted(df[column].unique()) # Obtaining unique categories

    for scenario in scenarios:
        group = folium.FeatureGroup(name=str(scenario))
        
        scenario_df = df[df[column] == scenario] # Filter by scenario
        
        means = scenario_df.groupby("location")["count"].mean().reset_index() # Groupby first because it is in long format. Then obtaining the mean and setting the index column to a normal column
        
        map_df = pd.merge(means, geo_df, left_on='location', right_on='Address') # Merging the data

        # Creating heat map for the scenario
        heat_data = [[row['Latitude'], row['Longitude'], row['count']] for _, row in map_df.iterrows()] 
        HeatMap(heat_data, radius=25, blur=15, gradient={0.4: 'blue', 0.65: 'lime', 1: 'red'}).add_to(group)
        
        if display_marker:
            # Add Markers to this scenario
            for _, row in map_df.iterrows():
                folium.CircleMarker(
                    location=[row['Latitude'], row['Longitude']],
                    radius=5, 
                    color='black',
                    fill=True,
                    fill_opacity=0.7,
                    popup=folium.Popup(f"<b>{scenario}</b><br>{row['location']}<br>Avg: {int(row['count'])}/hr", max_width=200)
                ).add_to(group)
        
        group.add_to(m)


    folium.LayerControl().add_to(m) # Adding the controls
    
    # m.save(f"Auckland_CBD_{column}_Map.html")
    return m
```

Similarly, the underlying pedestrian movement pattern remains similar with different wind and weather conditions.
```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count Spatial Map By Scenarios"

stress = display_spatial_map(working_hour_df, "weather_stress", geo_location, True)
stress
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count Spatial Map By Wind"

wind = display_spatial_map(working_hour_df, "wind", geo_location, True)
wind
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Average Pedestrian Count Spatial Map By Weather"

weather = display_spatial_map(working_hour_df, "weather", geo_location, True)
weather
```

It appears that my assumption is wrong, and I must admit defeat to Aucklanders. I guess if you are an Aucklander. You will always get to where you need to be if you want to, and it will take more than just the weather to stop you. Round of applause to Aucklanders.

# Final Remark

While the mean stays the same. But I think there is a hidden message behind the outliers. The outliers do not go as high as before. We can classify the pedestrian in Auckland CBD into two distinct groups:

1. The essential workers
This includes people who works in the Auckland CBD, including university students, or anyone who visits the Auckland CBD on a frequency basis.

2. The casual shoppers
This includes people who visits Auckland CBD on an occasional basis. Including tourist.

One potential explanation is that, the mean stays consistent is largely due to the essential workers group - people who have to go to Auckland CBD regardless of the weather situation. Whereas the weather factors only affects the casual shoppers. Which explains the significant drop in the outliers. Regardless, the Aucklanders has earned my respect for being unstoppable. 

# Technical Limitations

This study only analyzed the pedestrian dataset from 2024. Therefore the finding only applicable to 2024. This study does not apply to other years. In other years there may have been a relationship between pedestrian count and temperature/wind speed/weather variables. 

Open-Meteo only provides hourly weather data. But it doesn’t disclose how the hourly weather data is measured. For instance we do not know if it rained for the whole hour, or just for one minute. Given the typical “Auckland weather”. This could make a significant difference.

There may have been limitation on the sensor cameras. It may be inadequate to capture all the pedestrian in the street. There is also a possibility that it has double counted pedestrian, or is unable to pickup some pedestrians. 

The limited positions of the sensors may not provide us the whole picture. Certain locations may see a stronger correlation between the pedestrian count and temperature/wind speed/weather variables.


# Reproducibility
The code used for this analysis can be found in the GitHub repository:
https://github.com/jhe482-uoa/Auckland-CBD-Pedestrian-Analysis

A readme file can be found for the instructions on how to run the code.


# Code Walkthrough
A 10 minutes video below explaining the code:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GhpLjjLjCfw
